rows: (the crows have this one now)
(the gibbet creaks; nothing answers)
(dead men keep the best secrets on these docks)
Uhh... the stones... so cold...
Help a body up... or step over, like the rest...
(a wet cough) Not... done yet...
The tide... tell them the tide took my boots...
A day in the cage for nothing at all.
Tell the turnkey my wage is owed. He laughed. You laugh too, go on.
When I'm out, the Watch buys the first round. With my coin, likely.
The bars are King's iron. Finest thing I ever held.
Can't step past my own sill. Watch's orders.
House arrest, they call it. Starving under a roof, I call it.
Come to gawk at the door-bound? Bring bread next time.
Me and this wall are old friends now.
Out of my way! Out of it!
They're coming - d'you hear? They're COMING!
Run, fool! Why are you standing there?
Flame keep me, Flame keep me, Flame keep me...
Moving on, moving on. Boots don't stop in this ward.
The spear said walk, so I walk.
No rest for the warned.
Ask the Watch where a body's allowed to stand. Go on, ask.
Rope and tide, friend.
Another haul, another day.
Mind the lines there.
Work's work.
Supper's thin, but there's a bowl for you.
Home before dark, you hear?
Blood minds blood. Watch yourself on the quay.
There's my favorite face on this mudheap.
Still owe you that drink at the Gull.
Good to see you upright and unfined.
You've got sand, showing that face at the ropes.
Foreman says work beside you. Didn't say speak.
Get gone before the lads notice you.
Heard things about you. Keep to your side of the line.
My rope, my crate, my business. Yours is elsewhere.
(a grunt, and a turned shoulder)
Make way, make way - a friend of the quays.
You stood by us. The ropes remember.
First pull's yours if you want work, friend.
Mind the crates.
You lost, or just idle?
Tide's in, hands are short. Talk fast.
Dawn bell rang. Muster's forming on the quay.
Cold start. The rope's stiff and so am I.
Morning. Mind the gull mess on the planks.
Full press at the berths today.
No idling - the crane line's moving.
Day's half spent and the hold's still full.
Last haul, then the Gull for me.
Shoulders are done. Tongue's next, over ale.
Evening. The wage table can't come soon enough.
Hsst - you'll wake the bunkhouse.
Night shift's for the young and the indebted.
Late to be on the planks. Fall in and no one fishes you out.
Spare a Royal for a dry throat?
The strand gives, the strand takes.
You look flush. I look hungry. Fate's a jest.
Keep your purse deep and your questions shallow.
We share blood, so share a warning: the strand's picked clean today.
Cousin. The Mission pot's on at dusk - go early.
Family price on my advice: it's free, and it's 'run'.
There's a friendly face. Got any bread on you?
Sit. The step's free, which is the best price in the ward.
You I'd share a found Royal with. Maybe.
You. The gulls speak better of carrion.
Come closer and I'll scream thief. Who'd they believe, eh?
Strand law: what you did doesn't wash off.
Word crawls the gutters faster than you'd think. I heard it.
Keep your shadow off my patch.
(spits toward the gutter)
Friend of the street! Sit, sit - the stones are warmest here.
Feed the gutter and the gutter feeds you back. What d'you need?
For you? I saw nothing and I heard everything. Ask.
Got a Royal? Half a Royal? A crust?
The strand's free to walk. Walking's about all that is.
New face. Old story, I'd wager.
Tide left treasure somewhere. Just never here.
First light's for mudlarks and regrets.
Morning. The bakers dump the burnt loaves soon - don't crowd me.
Watch moves us along at noon. Keep shuffling.
Day crowd's stingy. Evening crowd drinks first and gives better.
You're standing in my sun.
Mission pot's on. You can smell hope from here.
Dusk, and the ward goes soft-hearted. Best hour to ask.
Evening. Spare what the day spared you?
Off the strand at night - the tide takes sleepers.
Night's for the Runners. I hold walls up, me.
You didn't see me. I'm a heap of nets, understand?
Business?
Keep it orderly.
The Watch sees you.
Walk on, citizen.
Shouldn't stop long - the sergeant watches me watch you.
Tell mother the wage lands on Kingsday.
Stay off the quay after dark. I mean it, blood or no.
If it isn't my favorite trouble.
Between us: patrols double at the warehouses tonight.
Keep your nose clean and my rounds boring, eh?
Give me a reason. Any reason.
The gibbet's had thinner necks than yours.
Every step you take in my ward, I count it.
You're on the list, you know.
The Watch sees you, gutter-blood.
One report says your name twice. Care to make it thrice?
Up early? Planning something, or fleeing it?
Dawn doesn't wash a name clean.
You again, and the day barely lit.
I'm watching you in broad daylight. Imagine the dark.
The sergeant knows your face. Keep it moving.
Noon post, and here's trouble already.
Dusk's when your sort goes to work, isn't it?
Lamps are lit. Stay under them where I can see you.
Evening. Your name came up at the post again.
Move along, you.
The Watch sees you, gutter-blood.
Wrong hour for a listed name to wander.
One shout from me and you sleep in K34. Walk.
Good day to you. Rare, saying that and meaning it.
The ward could use more of your sort.
Any trouble finds you, it answers to the post.
State your business.
Keep the lane clear.
Papers, cargo, or trouble - which are you?
Morning muster's done. Streets are mine till dusk.
Early. Honest folk and thieves both love this hour.
Move along - the dawn cart's coming through.
Busy hour. Keep your purse forward, citizen.
Midday post. All quiet, and I intend it stays so.
Mind the crane line - one flattened fool a season is plenty.
Dusk. The honest go home now, so what's keeping you?
Lamps are lit. So are the cutpurses' eyes.
Evening rounds. Walk with purpose or walk home.
Curfew's not law here yet. Don't make me wish it were.
Late walker. The gibbet started as a late walker too, I'd wager.
Name and business, and quick about it.
The Flame keep you.
Warmth of the Flame on you, child.
The Mission feeds at dusk. Tell the hungry.
Every soul a wick, every wick a light.
The Flame lights kin twice over. Come to the Mission after.
Blood of my blood. Have you eaten today - truly?
Mother asks for you at prayers. Don't make me lie again.
Friend. The pot's on early tonight - come hungry.
The Flame's warmer with you near it.
Sit with me at dusk-song. The bench misses you.
Even you the Flame would take back. But not through my door tonight.
I pray for you. From a distance.
The Flame sees what you did. It burns things clean - remember that.
The Flame forgives. I am still working on it.
Alms are for the hungry, not the grasping.
(a blessing, made quickly and without warmth)
The Flame's own friend! The Mission owes you a seat by the fire.
Blessings, child. Your kindness is spoken of at prayers.
Warm hands, warm heart, warm Flame. Come in, come in.
The Flame keep you.
Peace of the Flame on you, child.
Hungry? The Mission pot takes all comers at dusk.
Dawn-song's just done. The Flame is lit fresh.
Morning, child. The Flame rose before us all.
First light. Best light - the Flame's light.
The Flame keep your working hours.
Midday. Even the Flame rests low - eat something, child.
The Mission door stands open, as ever.
Dusk-song soon. Come, even just to warm your hands.
The pot's on. Bring your hunger and any news of the sick.
Evening, child. The Flame gutters but never goes.
The night is the Flame's oldest argument. Walk in its light.
Late, child. The Mission bunks are full, but the floor is dry.
The Flame keeps watch when the Watch sleeps.
Buying, or blocking my counter?
Stock's fresh as the tide brings.
A Royal spent here is a Royal well kept.
Take it off the shelf, but the family price is still a price.
Kin at the counter! Mind the till while I fetch the good stock.
Tell the house I'll close early for supper. Maybe.
For you, the scale reads honest.
My friend! Buy something, so the Watch thinks you're a customer.
The back shelf's got better. Friends know to ask.
Out. My counter serves the ward, not its rot.
Your coin's no good here. Nor your face.
I've a bell for the Watch and a bar for the door. Pick.
Coin first. Handle nothing.
I count my stock when your sort visits. Twice.
The Row talks, you know. About you.
Ah, good custom! The scale tips your way today.
For a friend of the Row, the fresh stock - not the front stock.
Your Royals ring honest. Rare music, that.
First customer of the day, and a welcome one at that.
Morning, friend of the Row. The bread's still warm somewhere under here.
For you I'd open early any day.
The good stock's yours to pick over, friend.
Busy day, but never too busy for good custom.
Your name stands well on the Row's books. Buy what you like.
Stay past the shutter - your coin's always welcome.
Evening, friend. The last of the fresh is yours before it turns.
For you, the counter stays open a breath longer.
For you? The shutter opens. Quietly, mind.
It's late, but a friend's knock is never trouble.
Quick now - what does a friend of the Row need at this hour?
Buying?
Stock's what you see. Price is what I say.
Mind the display. Broken is bought.
Fresh off the morning tide. Well - fresh enough.
Early custom's best custom. What'll it be?
Just opened. Scale's clean, till's empty, hopes are up.
Midday press - queue like decent folk.
Stock moves fast today. Decide faster.
Day rate's the fair rate. Ask anyone on the Row.
Closing soon. Last measures going cheap-ish.
Evening trade's thin. Make my day - buy twice.
The counter shuts at dark. The tab shuts never.
We're shut. The sign, the shutter and the bar all say so.
Night custom's trouble custom. What do you want?
Come back with the sun. Or knock the Watch's way, not mine.
Fair wind to you.
Tide waits on no one, so talk quick.
Salt in the air and no ship under me. A waste.
Watch the mooring lines, landsman.
Blood aboard! Mind the lines and stay off the rail.
Tell the house I sail on the next tide. And that I'm sorry.
Kin or no, the captain's word is weather aboard.
Ha! Thought the tide took you years back.
My old shipmate's shadow! Come drink the harbor dry.
For you I'd even haul rope sober.
We keel-haul your sort where I'm from.
The sea refuses some cargo. You're some cargo.
Step aboard and learn to swim the hard way.
I've heard what the harbor says about you.
Stand clear of the gangplank. And of me.
(makes the old sign against foul wind)
A friend of the crews! The Gull's first round is yours tonight.
You're good luck on a quay, they say. Stand closer.
Fair wind follows you, friend. Share it.
Fair wind.
Mind the lines, landsman.
Cargo or questions? I've patience for one.
Morning tide's the honest tide.
Dawn watch done. Deck's scrubbed, throat's dry.
Early bird gets the best berth.
Loading till dusk. Talk while I haul.
Midday sun and a full hold - a good day, that.
The crane waits for no conversation.
Evening tide's a smuggler's tide. Not that I'd know.
Last light. The Gull's lamps look warmer than my bunk.
Dusk. Wind's turning - feel it?
Night watch. Just me, the rats, and whatever you are.
Quiet hours on the quay. Keep them quiet.
The sea's loudest when the ward sleeps. Listen.
Mind the beasts.
Fed and watered, same as I wish I was.
A good dog's worth three men. Cheaper, too.
Wash your hands before supper if you've touched the pens. Mother's rule, my rule.
Kin visit! The goats eat before we talk - oldest law in the ward.
Blood helps muck stalls free. That's what blood's for.
The dogs like you. That's references enough for me.
Friend! Hold this feed sack and earn your gossip.
You're good with beasts. Rarer than good with people.
The dogs remember you. So do I.
Touch a beast of mine and lose the hand.
Even the goats won't take feed from your side of the fence.
Beasts judge quicker than the Watch. Mine judged you already.
Stand back from the pens. Things go missing when you're about, I hear.
(the dog at their heel growls low)
The pens are calmer when you're by. Come round any time.
Friend of the yards! The pups are weaned - come pick the bold one.
You've an honest smell about you. The beasts agree.
Mind the beasts.
Pens are private. Path's public. Know which is which.
Feed's paid for, beast by beast. No charity in a trough.
Feeding hour. Stand clear of the rush.
Morning. The beasts woke me before the bell, as ever.
Milking's done. The rest of the day's downhill.
Exercise rounds. The horse leads, we follow.
Midday's for mucking. Stand upwind, friend.
The kennels nap at noon. I envy them daily.
Last feed at dusk. Then the ward's on its own.
Penning up. Help, or step back.
Evening. The beasts hear the night coming before we do.
The dogs bark at nothing all night. Or not nothing - who knows.
Beasts are down. Keep your voice low.
Night check. A restless pen means a prowler about.
(it watches you sidelong)
(a low sound, neither welcome nor warning)
(it goes still as you pass)
(it noses at you, tail low but easy - it knows the household smell)
(a soft chuff of recognition)
(its tail beats once, twice - it remembers you kindly)
(it leans its warm weight against your leg)
(hackles rise; a long, low growl)
(it bares teeth - it has not forgotten)
(it backs off a pace, watching your hands)
(a wary circle, keeping the distance even)
(it trots over, hopeful of scraps)
(an easy, open-mouthed pant - you rank among the feeders)
(it watches you sidelong)
(a sniff; you are catalogued and dismissed)
(it goes still as you pass)
(it stretches long, jaws wide in a dawn yawn)
(it stirs from a scrap-pile nest, blinking at the light)
(it dozes in a strip of sun, one eye on the traffic)
(it noses along the gutter line, working)
(it grows restless as the lamps take)
(it patrols its patch with the dusk shift)
(eyes catch the lamplight, twin coins in the dark)
(a shape detaches from deeper shadow, considers you, returns)
Speak, if you must.
The ward burns slow. I am watching it.
Few stop me to talk. Fewer twice.
You I keep close. Stay close.
Blood is a small fire that must not go out.
Walk with me a while. The ward talks less when I have company.
Few names sit easy with me. Yours does.
Do not test what I carry.
You mistake patience for mercy. Correct that.
I know what is said of you. Convince me it is wrong.
Stand where I can see your hands.
The ward speaks well of you. That is rarer than gold here.
You have been useful. Remain so, and prosper.
Speak, if you must.
Make it brief. The ward does not watch itself.
Yes?
The day is young and already owed.
Dawn. What the night hid, the morning sells.
The ward works. Working wards keep their secrets shallow.
Speak while I walk.
Dusk gathers the honest home and turns the rest loose.
The lamps take. So do the lampless. Speak.
The night is mine to walk. State your reason for sharing it.
Softly. The dark has ears it did not buy.
(they size you up in silence)
(a nod, no more - you are known here, or soon will be)
The Weighhouse scale is the only honest tongue on this harbor.
Ask your question as you'd load a hold: heaviest first.
Every crate that crosses my quay has a number. Most have the true one.
The King taxes what I weigh. What I don't weigh, the King doesn't miss.
You read ledgers? Then you know a clean page can lie.
Saltgate stays quiet because I am not.
I've buried three quiet weeks this year. Each one ended loud.
The magistrate prices a broken head at two Royals. I price it differently.
Some doors in this ward I watch on my own time.
The Gull pours and I remember. That's the whole arrangement.
The Lantern Room hears every tide's worth of grief. It keeps all of it.
Sit. The kettle's always near the fire.
What the harbor takes, we name in the smoke.
The Flame does not ask where you slept. Neither do I.
The pot is shallow tonight, but it has never once been empty.
I came to these docks carrying worse debts than any wastrel here.
Grief is a wet log. It smokes long before it warms. Sit anyway.
The Lantern Room and I keep different vigils for the same souls.
The pot won't stir itself. Talk while I work.
Everyone's hungry for something. Soup's just the honest one.
Father Maell prays for the ward. I salt the broth. Same work.
A dog's growl tells you more truth than a week of tavern talk.
I've kenneled for this ward since before the Terrace had walls.
Rat-dogs earn their keep in the King's own coin. Can't say that of every man here.
The impound keeps beasts FROM men. I keep beasts FOR them. Cull forgets the difference.
Feed a dog, keep a friend. Cheapest loyalty on the docks.
The strand gives up a treasure a tide, if you know the mud.
Fenner's hook is in every find I pull. One day the tide pays HIM back.
I saw what the water did last spring. I don't say it after dark.
The Wrackhouse buys what the sea's done chewing. No questions - that's the trade.
Salvage law's the oldest law on this coast. Older than the King's.
Brann calls my stock stolen. The sea doesn't issue receipts.
The yard builds true. What it builds after dark is the yard's business.
Timber, tar and silence - my three trades.
Some cargoes never touch the Weighhouse books. I whistle while they pass.
Chandlery's honest work: rope, tallow, tar, and no surprises.
Half the Wrackhouse stock floated off MY manifests, I'd swear to it.
A ship provisions here, or it provisions poorly.
The Gilded Gull waters no ale before the third round.
Everything said in my taproom stays in my taproom. Priced by the keg.
Sergeants drink free. Cheaper than fines, dearer than friends.
Pawn's a mercy trade. Ask anyone holding my paper.
Everything in this window has a story. The interest is for the ending.
Wages advance, debts remain. It's the remaining I'm good at.
The ferry runs when I say the water's kind.
Every soul this waterman's carried is a fare. Some are also a secret.
The channel's moods pay my bread. I've learned all nine of them.
Hot water's the one luxury the whole docks agrees on.
What's said in the steam stays in the steam. Mostly.
Even the Watch unbuckles here. You'd be amazed what floats out of a soaked man.
The Kestrel's fast because I keep her hungry.
A captain's word is cargo too. Mine's dear.
The Flame gets a tithe each crossing. Storms respect bookkeeping.
Bregga's Promise keeps hers. Full holds, fair weights.
The Row trusts my manifest over the Weighhouse scale. Draw your own conclusions.
Twenty years of crossings and the sea still hasn't collected.
The Deep Keel takes the cargo others won't. Price of being last to the quay.
The Row's rates would beach an honest ship. So I'm not always honest.
Deep water forgives nothing. Neither do I.
House Quayward laid the first stone of this quay. Remember that when the rents come due.
Old money isn't quiet money. It's patient money.
The harbor works for the houses. It only thinks otherwise.
The Netters' nets fed this ward before it had a name.
A widow learns exactly what a promise weighs.
The house stands. Whatever you've heard, the house stands.
Saltgate Terrace was built on salt and stubbornness. We've plenty of both left.
Good fences, they say. My neighbors test the saying.
A goodman's word, once given, is stone. Think before you ask for it.
The Counting-House holds no secrets. It holds collateral.
A Royal is a promise the King makes. I merely keep his word for him.
Interest is patience, priced.
Men lie. Ledgers lie better, but they need help to do it.
The vault door is honest iron. Everything before it is negotiation.
Me? I keep to my room, mostly.
The roofs? Loud with gulls. I sleep through it.
Folk pay me no mind, which suits.
I hear things through the floor. Most of it's not worth the hearing.
Quiet tenants make good neighbors. That's all I am.
The Bilge serves whoever the night washes in.
No names below the waterline - house rule.
The Watch drinks upstairs. Everyone else drinks honest.
The Ropewalk turns hemp to rigging for half the coast.
A rope's a thousand small twists holding one big weight. So's a crew.
Walk the full walk if you like - three hundred paces of honest work.
Pitchfield smokes because ships must not leak. Simple as that.
You smell it? That's the smell of nobody drowning.
Fire and tar are old partners. I just keep them civil.
Salt Row cures what the boats bring, or the ward eats it rotten.
Salt's the only wage that never spoils.
My barrels outlast governments. Ask my grandmother's.
The King's Bond holds what the law says it holds.
Surety is a cold trade with warm margins.
Bonded, seized or forfeit - everything in here is one of the three.
The Rows shelter more souls than the Terrace ever will.
Rent's due on the ebb. Every ebb.
Keeper of the Rows means keeper of a hundred quarrels. I charge for neither.
The Arcade's nets pass through these hands twice a season, every season.
Grandmother, they call me. I've outlived three harbormasters and two piers.
The Netmenders see every hull that limps in. We mend more than nets, dear.
A true barrel is a small ship. Mind who you say otherwise to.
The cooperage banded this ward's trade before the Bank counted it.
Oak remembers. So do I.
The Loft cuts canvas for three fleets and mends for a fourth.
Wind's free. Catching it is what costs.
A sail's just a wall the sky agrees to push.
Hardtack outlasts weevils, weather and kings.
The oven's older than the Terrace. Bakes truer, too.
Soft bread's for the hill. Down here we bake for the sea.
Slops for every back and boots for most feet - dead men's sizes run cheap.
The chest takes trade-ins. Don't ask whose.
Warm and dry is the whole of dockside fashion.
The Long Store swallows cargo whole and gives it back by the chit.
Every aisle numbered, every number mine.
Lose your chit and your goods learn new owners. Store law.
The impound holds strays, seized stock and the odd evidence-beast.
Watchman first, keeper second. The beasts prefer it that way, oddly.
Cobb coddles his kennels. Mine EARN the feed.
The goats know this ward better than the Watch does.
Milk, cheese and gossip - the pen trades in all three.
My nannies have eaten three eviction notices. Good girls.
The dray goes where the crane can't. That's most places, truth told.
A carter hears every quarrel on his route twice - once going, once coming.
Feed the horse before you flatter the man.
The Hold whispers on the ebb. I'm the only one polite enough to listen.
Drowned men keep excellent ledgers. I've seen them balance.
Mad, they say. The water says otherwise. The water says PLENTY.
The Gullet keeps its own. The ward forgot us; we returned the favor.
Gray voice, they call me. Gray sees what color misses.
Every ruin was somebody's fortune. Sit on that a while.
The guardhouse holds the line the Terrace pretends isn't there.
Vess polishes Saltgate. Someone has to garrison the REAL wall.
K34's cells are full or filling. Efficiency, that.
Room at the top of the stair's not to let. It's PAID, and it stays paid.
Lodgers come asking after the quiet room. I tell them the quiet room has a tenant.
He'd read me the day's figures over supper. I don't eat late anymore.
The Watch wrote his name once and never again. I write it every rent-day.
Sweep, trim the lamp, turn the key. A room forgets a man faster than a grave does.
The ward talks. Mostly true, always late.
You hear things on a quay. Wind, mostly. Mostly.
Ask the bathhouse or the Bilge - all news steams loose eventually.
Brann swears the Wrackhouse sells his own stock back to him. Dagny swears the sea's a fair merchant.
Chandler and wracker, at it again. The quay bets on Dagny.
They say half Brann's manifests wash up in Dagny's yard with the labels sanded off.
Fenner's got his hook in the mudlark - every tide-find goes through the pawn first.
Poor Jek. Finds treasure weekly, owns none of it by supper.
Neddry's slop-chest runs on Fenner's paper now, they say. Stock and all.
The Slop-Chest's shelves are Fenner's shelves, past the third hook anyway.
They whisper the Widow Netter signed paper at Fenner's window. The HOUSE, some say.
The Netters' nets are older than the ward, and mortgaged newer than the paint.
Vess and the Gull's landlord scrape like hull and quay. Something about the back room.
The sergeant drinks anywhere but the Gilded Gull. Ask Venn why, then watch him smile wrong.
One line in the Weighhouse book got scraped clean, and the banker's blotter knows which.
Crell weighs, Gilt counts, and between them one number never happened.
Harl's yard unloads Quayward lighters by night, and his whistling is PAID whistling.
Ask Harl what came off the midnight lighter. Ask him twice - the silence is the answer.
The Netters and Saltgate Terrace feud over a wall two spans wide. Lawyers fatten on it yearly.
That party wall's cost both houses more than a new mansion. Pride's dear masonry.
Gullet Mag keeps an odd friend - that quiet tenant from up the terraces. Odd pair.
Mag's gray voice goes soft for exactly one lodger. Wonder what she knows.
Two sergeants, one captaincy coming. Saltgate and the guardhouse don't share a table.
Vess writes reports. Brakk fills cells. The magistrate reads only one of them.
Cobb and Cull have feuded since a pup went to the wrong kennel twenty years back.
Kennel Row and the Impound agree on nothing. Not even the feed merchant.
Mother Sethra and Father Maell were promised once, before the Flame took both. Ask no one.
Two vigils, one grief, forty years. The Lantern Room and the Mission never quite face each other.
The mudlark leaves tide-glass at the Mission step. The soup's thicker when he does.
Onna saves the marrow bone for one bowl in particular. The ward pretends not to see.
Salt Row orders more barrels than fish lately. The cooper delivers each one himself.
Stave's best oak goes to Salla's row. Priced in smiles, the apprentices say.
The Loft's lamp burns late whenever the Bilge's does. Coincidence, twice a week, for a year.
Redda keeps a sailmaker's needle behind the bar. Luff keeps a tankard on his cutting bench.
The clerk? Aye, everyone's heard. No one's LOOKED.
A man vanished the same night as a number. On these docks that's called arithmetic.
Ask at the Weighhouse. Crell never stopped asking.
The counting-house clerk? He came to the night-pot Firstdays. Paid in coin, stayed for the warmth. Then he didn't.
He'd sit by the door with his soup, doing sums in the steam on the window. The last night he was here he kept looking toward the Weighhouse. Ask Harbormaster Crell.
I kept his bowl by a week. Father says hope's not a sin. Mine went cold with the soup.
Tide brought me a clerk's cuff-button, brass, the week after he went. The strand keeps better records than the Watch.
Saw him his last evening, up on the quay wall - walking Weighhouse-way, fast, papers under his arm. Crell's door. Start there.
Men go in the water loose or carried. The strand never gave me HIM back, so draw your own line.
The Wrackhouse logs what the sea returns. A whole clerk never came through. His WRITING did - a salvage chit in his neat hand, for a crate nobody ever claimed.
He copied tariff papers for me once. Fair hand, honest count. The Weighhouse knew his worth - ask Crell what the boy was worth to HIM.
No body through my door, and I see most of them. Whatever happened, the sea didn't do it.
Tam. His name is Tam Sedge, my brother's son, and you're the first to ask it in a season.
He went out after supper with his good coat and his pen-case and not his lamp. You don't take a pen-case to the water.
The Watch wrote him down once and never came back. Harbormaster Crell came TWICE. Go to the Weighhouse; start there.
His room is paid. It stays paid till I know.
So someone finally asks. One line was scraped out of my ledger, and the clerk who copied it stopped existing the same night. I want the copy. I want the LINE.
The boy kept a fair-copy of everything he inked - a habit I taught him. If it's anywhere, it's in his desk at the Counting-House. Gilt has let no one near it.
I can't pass Gilt's spears without making it a war of offices. You, though - you're nobody's office. His desk. The west wall of the hall.
The hall answers to the Watch's colors, not mine. Wear a face the door respects, or be quick between the spears' patience.
Gilt keeps every key on his own belt. Every key. One belt. I say no more.
The hall is for custom, not for standing about. State your business or the spears will state it for you.
The desk by the west wall? Estate property, pending audit. Its late owner's affairs are the Counting-House's affairs. Good day.
You are the third person this season to glance at that desk. The other two worked here. Neither does now.
That's his hand. That's the LINE. Give it to me and the scale weighs it where the whole quay can watch.
Raise it with me and the Watch stands with you after. Bury it and you're Gilt's - priced, shelved and owned.
Ah. The fair-copy. I did wonder where the boy kept it. Forty Royals says the number stays never-happened, and the Counting-House remembers a friend.
Crell would make it a trial. Trials weigh everyone they touch. Forty Royals weighs nothing at all.
A number a banker paid to bury? That's not paper, friend. That's rent. The roofs collect on it forever.
Give it up the wall and the brotherhood banks it. Nobody raises it, nobody burns it. It just... earns.

notes: Sprint-2 WORLD bark text tables (Trojian voice, docks register). Key vocabulary is BarkSelector's contract: mood.<state> and greet.<family>.<attitude>.<time> with the documented sparse fallback (a.b.c.d -> a.b.c -> a.b). Every family x attitude pair is authored; time-band refinements cover every family's neutral lane plus watch.cold and trade.warm. personal.<notableId> (all notables; client-selected when the speaker is a notable) and gossip.<historyId> (one per authored micro-history in names/histories.json, base 'gossip' as the fallback floor) are World key families layered on the same registry. S3 adds quest.<questId>[.<stageKey>[.<partySymbol>]] (quests/quests.json vocabulary; the talk surface's quest-beat lane, base quest.<questId> as the fallback floor) - linted stage-by-party by DocksBarkContentTest. Plain ASCII only (Glyph8x8Font); beast rows are observer stage directions in parentheses.
